eel: noun. Any freshwater fish of the order Anguilliformes, which are elongated and resemble snakes.
eely: adjective. Resembling an eel: long, thin and slippery.
else: adjective. Other; in addition to previously mentioned items.
ers: verb. third-person singular simple present indicative of er
eve: noun. The day or night before, usually used for holidays, such as Christmas Eve.
every: determiner. All of a countable group (considered individually), without exception.
eye: noun. An organ through which animals see (“perceive surroundings via light”).
eyer: noun. One who eyes someone or something.
eyre: noun. A journey taken by certain Medieval English itinerant judges (justices in eyre)
lee: noun. A protected cove or harbor, out of the wind.
leer: verb. To look sideways or obliquely; now especially with sexual desire or malicious intent.
leery: adjective. Cautious, suspicious, wary, hesitant, or nervous about something; having reservations or concerns.
lees: noun. The sediment that settles during fermentation of beverages, consisting of dead yeast and precipitated parts of the fruit.
lev: noun. The currency of Bulgaria from 1880 to 2025; divided into 100 stotinki.
levee: noun. An elevated ridge of deposited sediment on the banks of a river, formed by the river's overflow at times of high discharge.
lever: noun. A rigid piece which is capable of turning about one point, or axis (fulcrum), and in which are two or more other points where forces
levy: verb. To impose (a tax or fine) to collect monies due, or to confiscate property.
ley: noun. Arable land used temporarily for hay or grazing.
lye: noun. An alkaline liquid made by leaching ashes (usually wood ashes).
lyre: noun. An ancient stringed musical instrument (a yoke lute chordophone) of Greek origin, consisting of two arms extending from a body to a
lyse: verb. To burst or cut a cell or cell structure; to induce lysis.
ree: noun. Alternative form of rei (“Portuguese real”).
reel: noun. A shaky or unsteady gait.
reeve: noun. Any of several local officials, with varying responsibilities.
rely: verb. To trust; to have confidence in; to depend.
resee: verb. To see again or anew.
rev: verb. To increase the speed of a motor, or to operate at a higher speed.
revel: noun. An instance of merrymaking; a celebration.
rye: noun. A grain used extensively in Europe for making bread, beer, and (now generally) for animal fodder.
see: verb. To perceive or detect someone or something with the eyes, or as if by sight.
seely: adjective. Alternative form of sely or silly.
seer: noun. One who foretells the future; a clairvoyant, prophet, soothsayer or diviner.
sel: symbol. ISO 639-2 & ISO 639-3 language code for Selkup.
ser: noun. An old Indian unit of weight, equal to 80 tolas, or one fortieth of a maund.
serve: noun. An act of putting the ball or shuttlecock in play in various games.
sleeve: noun. The part of a garment that covers the arm.
sly: adjective. Artfully cunning; secretly mischievous; wily.
slyer: adjective. comparative form of sly: more sly
vee: noun. The name of the Latin script letter V / v.
veer: verb. To let out (a sail-line), to allow (a sheet) to run out.
veery: noun. An American thrush (Catharus fuscescens) common in the Northern United States and Canada.
verse: noun. A poetic form with regular meter and a fixed rhyme scheme.
very: adverb. To a great extent or degree.
yes: particle. Used to show agreement or acceptance.
eeler: noun. A fisherman who catches eels.
eelery: noun. A place where eels are caught.
everse: verb. to evert (overthrow, subvert, or turn inside out)
lese: verb. Alternative form of leese (“to lose”)
reese: noun. Synonym of peanut butter cup
sey: symbol. ISO 639-3 language code for Secoya.
sleever: noun. A person employed to fit sleeves to garments.
vly: noun. A small swampy or marshy area, especially near a stream.
yee: pronoun. You (the people being addressed); alternative spelling of ye.
yere: pronoun. your (plural); of ye, belonging to ye
